| | --- | | * [Checking](/wiki/Transactional_account "Transactional account") * [Money-market](/wiki/Money_market_account "Money market account") * [Savings](/wiki/Savings_account "Savings account") * [Certificate of deposit](/wiki/Certificate_of_deposit "Certificate of deposit") | | Account insurance | | [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC)](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation")  [National Credit Union Share Insurance Fund (NCUA)](/wiki/National_Credit_Union_Share_Insurance_Fund "National Credit Union Share Insurance Fund") |

| | --- | | * [Check](/wiki/Cheque#United_States "Cheque")   + [clearing](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_System#Check_clearing_system "Federal Reserve System")   + [Check 21 Act](/wiki/Check_21_Act "Check 21 Act") | | [Electronic funds transfer (EFT)](/wiki/Electronic_funds_transfer "Electronic funds transfer") | | * [ATM card](/wiki/ATM_card "ATM card") * [Debit card](/wiki/Debit_card "Debit card") * [Wire transfer](/wiki/Wire_transfer "Wire transfer") * [Substitute check](/wiki/Substitute_check "Substitute check")   * [ACH Network](/wiki/ACH_Network "ACH Network") * [Bill payment](/wiki/Electronic_bill_payment "Electronic bill payment") |

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Banking_in_the_United_States "Template:Banking in the United States") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Banking_in_the_United_States "Template talk:Banking in the United States") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Banking_in_the_United_States "Special:EditPage/Template:Banking in the United States")

(often shortened to the **Federal Reserve**, or simply **the Fed**) is the [central banking](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") system of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). It was created on December 23, 1913, with the enactment of the [Federal Reserve Act](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act "Federal Reserve Act"), after a series of [financial panics](/wiki/Financial_panic "Financial panic") (particularly the [panic of 1907](/wiki/Panic_of_1907 "Panic of 1907")) led to the desire for central control of the monetary system in order to alleviate financial crises.[[list 1]](#cite_note-need-11) Although an instrument of the U.S. government, the Federal Reserve System considers itself "an independent central bank because its monetary policy decisions do not have to be approved by the president or by anyone else in the executive or legislative branches of government, it does not receive funding appropriated by Congress, and the terms of the members of the board of governors span multiple presidential and congressional terms."[[11]](#cite_note-auto-12) Over the years, events such as the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") in the 1930s and the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") during the 2000s have led to the expansion of the roles and responsibilities of the Federal Reserve System.[[6]](#cite_note-initial-6)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

The **Federal Reserve System** (often shortened to the **Federal Reserve**, or simply **the Fed**) is the [central banking](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") system of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). It was created on December 23, 1913, with the enactment of the [Federal Reserve Act](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act "Federal Reserve Act"), after a series of [financial panics](/wiki/Financial_panic "Financial panic") (particularly the [panic of 1907](/wiki/Panic_of_1907 "Panic of 1907")) led to the desire for central control of the monetary system in order to alleviate financial crises.[[list 1]](#cite_note-need-11) Although an instrument of the U.S. government, the Federal Reserve System considers itself "an independent central bank because its monetary policy decisions do not have to be approved by the president or by anyone else in the executive or legislative branches of government, it does not receive funding appropriated by Congress, and the terms of the members of the board of governors span multiple presidential and congressional terms."[[11]](#cite_note-auto-12) Over the years, events such as the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") in the 1930s and the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") during the 2000s have led to the expansion of the roles and responsibilities of the Federal Reserve System.[[6]](#cite_note-initial-6)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

[Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress") established three key objectives for [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") in the Federal Reserve Act: maximizing employment, stabilizing prices, and moderating long-term interest rates.[[13]](#cite_note-14) The first two objectives are sometimes referred to as the Federal Reserve's dual mandate.[[14]](#cite_note-15) Its duties have expanded over the years, and include supervising and [regulating banks](/wiki/Bank_regulation "Bank regulation"), maintaining the stability of the financial system, and providing financial services to [depository institutions](/wiki/Depository_institution "Depository institution"), the U.S. government, and foreign official institutions.[[15]](#cite_note-mission-16) The Fed also conducts research into the economy and provides numerous publications, such as the [Beige Book](/wiki/Beige_Book "Beige Book") and the [FRED database](/wiki/FRED_database "FRED database").[[16]](#cite_note-17)

The Federal Reserve System is composed of several layers. It is governed by the presidentially appointed [board of governors](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Board_of_Governors "Federal Reserve Board of Governors") or Federal Reserve Board (FRB). Twelve regional [Federal Reserve Banks](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank "Federal Reserve Bank"), located in cities throughout the nation, regulate and oversee privately owned commercial banks.[[17]](#cite_note-18) Nationally chartered commercial banks are required to hold stock in, and can elect some board members of, the Federal Reserve Bank of their region.

The [Federal Open Market Committee](/wiki/Federal_Open_Market_Committee "Federal Open Market Committee") (FOMC) sets monetary policy by adjusting the target for the [federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate"), which generally influences [market interest rates](/wiki/Market_interest_rate "Market interest rate") and, in turn, US economic activity via the [monetary transmission mechanism](/wiki/Monetary_transmission_mechanism "Monetary transmission mechanism"). The FOMC consists of all seven members of the board of governors and the twelve regional Federal Reserve Bank presidents, though only five bank presidents vote at a time: the president of the [New York Fed](/wiki/New_York_Fed "New York Fed") and four others who rotate through one-year voting terms. There are also various advisory councils.[[list 2]](#cite_note-pubpriv-24) It has a structure unique among central banks, and is also unusual in that the [United States Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury"), an entity outside of the central bank, prints the [currency](/wiki/Currency "Currency") used.[[23]](#cite_note-25)

The federal government sets the salaries of the board's seven governors, and it receives all the system's annual profits after dividends on member banks' capital investments are paid, and an account surplus is maintained. In 2015, the Federal Reserve earned a net income of $100.2 billion and transferred $97.7 billion to the U.S. Treasury,[[24]](#cite_note-frs_bog_net_income_press_lease-26) and 2020 earnings were approximately $88.6 billion with remittances to the U.S. Treasury of $86.9 billion.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

The Federal Reserve [has been criticized](/wiki/Criticism_of_the_Federal_Reserve "Criticism of the Federal Reserve") for its approach to managing [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation"), perceived lack of transparency, and its role in economic downturns.[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30) Figures such as [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman") and [Ron Paul](/wiki/Ron_Paul "Ron Paul") have argued that its actions, such as [expansionary policies](/wiki/Expansionary_policies "Expansionary policies") and bailouts, contribute to inflation, [asset bubbles](/wiki/Economic_bubble "Economic bubble"), and [moral hazard](/wiki/Moral_hazard "Moral hazard").[[29]](#cite_note-:0-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33) Critics have also noted that the shift from the [gold standard](/wiki/Gold_standard#In_the_United_States "Gold standard") to [fiat currency](/wiki/Fiat_money "Fiat money") has led to long-term inflation and financial instability, with some calling for the [Fed's abolition](/wiki/End_the_Fed "End the Fed") or greater accountability through audits.[[32]](#cite_note-:1-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35)

## Purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Purpose")]

Before the founding of the Federal Reserve System, the United States underwent several financial crises. A particularly severe crisis in 1907 led Congress to enact the [Federal Reserve Act](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act "Federal Reserve Act") in 1913. The primary declared motivation for creating the Federal Reserve System was to address [banking panics](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run").[[6]](#cite_note-initial-6) Other stated purposes, are "to furnish an elastic currency, to afford means of rediscounting [commercial paper](/wiki/Commercial_paper "Commercial paper"), [and] to establish a more effective supervision of banking in the United States".[[34]](#cite_note-36)

Today, the purposes of the Federal Reserve include the responsibilities to:[[15]](#cite_note-mission-16)[[35]](#cite_note-BoG_2006_pp=1-37)

* Address the problem of [banking panics](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run")
* Serve as the [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") for the United States
* Balance private interests of banks and the centralized responsibility of government
  + Supervise and regulate banking institutions
  + Protect the credit rights of consumers
* Conduct [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") by influencing [market interest rates](/wiki/Market_interest_rate "Market interest rate") to achieve the goals of
  + Maximum employment
  + Stable prices, interpreted as an [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") rate of 2 percent per year on average[[36]](#cite_note-goals_of_MP-38)
  + Moderate long-term interest rates
* Maintain the stability of the financial system and contain [systemic risk](/wiki/Systemic_risk "Systemic risk") in financial markets
* Provide financial services to depository institutions, the U.S. government, and foreign official institutions
  + Facilitate the exchange of payments among regions and the operation of the nation's payments system
  + Respond to local liquidity needs
* Strengthen U.S. standing in the world economy

### Fractional-reserve bank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Fractional-reserve bank")]

Further information: [Bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") and [Fractional-reserve banking](/wiki/Fractional-reserve_banking "Fractional-reserve banking")

Banks usually invest the majority of the funds received from depositors. However, banking institutions in the United States are required to hold reserves‍—‌amounts of currency and deposits in other banks‍—‌equal to a fraction of the amount of the bank's deposit liabilities owed to customers. This practice is called [fractional-reserve banking](/wiki/Fractional-reserve_banking "Fractional-reserve banking"). On rare occasions, too many of the bank's customers will withdraw their savings such that the bank cannot continue operating on its own; this is called a [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run"). Bank runs can lead to a multitude of social and economic problems. The Federal Reserve System was designed as an attempt to prevent or minimize the occurrence of bank runs, and possibly act as a [lender of last resort](/wiki/Lender_of_last_resort "Lender of last resort") when a bank run occurs. Many economists, following [Nobel](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_Memorial_Prize_laureates_in_Economics "List of Nobel Memorial Prize laureates in Economics") laureate [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman"), believe that the Federal Reserve inappropriately refused to lend money to small banks during the bank runs of 1929; Friedman argued that this contributed to the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[37]](#cite_note-Federal_Reserve_Board-39)

#### Check clearing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Check clearing system")]

Before the establishment of the Federal Reserve, during times of economic uncertainty, some banks refused to [clear checks](/wiki/Cheque_clearing "Cheque clearing") from certain other banks, which led to large-scale [bank failure](/wiki/Bank_failure "Bank failure") in the early 20th-century. Hence, a national check-clearing system was created in the Federal Reserve System.[[38]](#cite_note-40) The Federal Reserve can physically accept and transport cheques.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

#### Lender of last resort

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Lender of last resort")]

In the United States of America, the Federal Reserve serves as the [lender of last resort](/wiki/Lender_of_last_resort "Lender of last resort") to those institutions that cannot obtain credit elsewhere, institutions the collapse of which would have serious implications for the economy. It took over this role from the private sector [clearing houses](/wiki/Clearing_house_(finance) "Clearing house (finance)") which operated during the [Free Banking Era](/wiki/History_of_central_banking_in_the_United_States#1837–1862:_"Free_Banking"_Era "History of central banking in the United States"). The availability of liquidity is intended to prevent bank runs.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

#### Fluctuations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Fluctuations")]

Reserve Banks provide liquidity to banks to meet short-term needs stemming from seasonal fluctuations in deposits or unexpected withdrawals through its [discount window](/wiki/Discount_window "Discount window") and [credit operations](/wiki/Loan "Loan"). Longer-term liquidity may also be provided in exceptional circumstances. The rate the Fed charges banks for these loans is called the discount rate (officially the primary credit rate). By making these loans, the Fed serves as a buffer against unexpected day-to-day fluctuations in reserve demand and supply. This contributes to the effective functioning of the banking system, alleviates pressure in the reserves market, and reduces the extent of unexpected movements in the interest rates.[[41]](#cite_note-dfeverydayecon-43) For example, on September 16, 2008, the Federal Reserve Board authorized an $85 billion loan to stave off the bankruptcy of international insurance giant [American International Group](/wiki/American_International_Group "American International Group") (AIG).[[42]](#cite_note-Federal_Reserve-AIG_Press_Release-2008-09-16-44)

### Central bank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Central bank")]

Further information: [Central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Obverse_of_the_series_2021_%241_Federal_Reserve_Note.jpg/250px-Obverse_of_the_series_2021_%241_Federal_Reserve_Note.jpg)](/wiki/File:Obverse_of_the_series_2021_$1_Federal_Reserve_Note.jpg)

Obverse of a Federal Reserve [$1 note](/wiki/United_States_one-dollar_bill "United States one-dollar bill") issued in 2021

As the [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") of the United States, the Fed serves as a [banker's bank](/wiki/Bankers%27_bank "Bankers' bank") and as the government's bank. As the banker's bank, it helps to assure the safety and efficiency of the payments system. As the government's bank or [fiscal agent](/wiki/Fiscal_agent "Fiscal agent"), the Fed processes a variety of financial transactions involving trillions of dollars. The [U.S. Treasury](/wiki/U.S._Treasury "U.S. Treasury") keeps a checking account with the Federal Reserve, through which incoming federal tax deposits and outgoing government payments are handled. As part of this service relationship, the Fed sells and redeems [U.S. government securities](/wiki/Treasury_security "Treasury security") such as savings bonds and Treasury bills, notes and bonds. It also issues the nation's [coin](/wiki/Coins_of_the_United_States_dollar "Coins of the United States dollar") and [paper currency](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_note "Federal Reserve note"). The U.S. Treasury, through its [Bureau of the Mint](/wiki/Bureau_of_the_Mint "Bureau of the Mint") and [Bureau of Engraving and Printing](/wiki/Bureau_of_Engraving_and_Printing "Bureau of Engraving and Printing"), actually produces the nation's cash supply and, in effect, sells the paper currency to the Federal Reserve Banks at manufacturing cost, and the coins at face value. The Federal Reserve Banks then distribute it to other financial institutions in various ways.[[43]](#cite_note-Fed_currency-45) During the [Fiscal Year](/wiki/Fiscal_Year "Fiscal Year") 2020, the Bureau of Engraving and Printing delivered 57.95 billion notes at an average cost of 7.4 cents per note.[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47)

#### Federal funds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Federal funds")]

Main article: [Federal funds](/wiki/Federal_funds "Federal funds")

Federal funds, officially [Federal Reserve Deposits](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Deposits "Federal Reserve Deposits"), are the reserve balances that private banks keep at their local Federal Reserve Bank.[[46]](#cite_note-48) These balances are the namesake reserves of the Federal Reserve System. The purpose of keeping funds at a Federal Reserve Bank is to have a mechanism for private banks to lend funds to one another. This market for funds plays an important role in the Federal Reserve System as it is the basis for its [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") work. Monetary policy is put into effect partly by influencing how much interest the private banks charge each other for the lending of these funds. Federal reserve accounts contain federal reserve credit, which can be converted into [federal reserve notes](/wiki/Federal_reserve_note "Federal reserve note").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Bank regulator

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Bank regulator")]

The Federal Reserve regulates private banks. The system was designed out of a compromise between the competing philosophies of privatization and government regulation. In 2006 [Donald L. Kohn](/wiki/Donald_L._Kohn "Donald L. Kohn"), vice chairman of the board of governors, summarized the history of this compromise:[[47]](#cite_note-49)

> Agrarian and progressive interests, led by William Jennings Bryan, favored a central bank under public, rather than banker, control. However, the vast majority of the nation's bankers, concerned about government intervention in the banking business, opposed a central bank structure directed by political appointees.
> The legislation that Congress ultimately adopted in 1913 reflected a hard-fought battle to balance these two competing views and created the hybrid public-private, centralized-decentralized structure that we have today.

In the structure of the Federal Reserve System, private banks elect members of the board of directors at their regional Federal Reserve Bank while the members of the board of governors are selected by the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and confirmed by the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate").[[48]](#cite_note-50)

#### Government regulation and supervision

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Government regulation and supervision")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/House_Financial_Services_Committee_hearing_with_Ben_Bernanke.jpg/250px-House_Financial_Services_Committee_hearing_with_Ben_Bernanke.jpg)](/wiki/File:House_Financial_Services_Committee_hearing_with_Ben_Bernanke.jpg)

[Ben Bernanke](/wiki/Ben_Bernanke "Ben Bernanke") (lower right), former chairman of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, at a [House Financial Services Committee](/wiki/House_Financial_Services_Committee "House Financial Services Committee") hearing on February 10, 2009. Members of the board frequently testify before congressional committees such as this one. The Senate equivalent of the House Financial Services Committee is the [Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs](/wiki/Senate_Committee_on_Banking,_Housing,_and_Urban_Affairs "Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs").

The Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System has a number of supervisory and regulatory responsibilities in the U.S. banking system, but not complete responsibility. A general description of the types of regulation and supervision involved in the U.S. banking system is given by the Federal Reserve:[[49]](#cite_note-51)

> The Board also plays a major role in the supervision and regulation of the U.S. banking system. It has supervisory responsibilities for state-chartered banks[[50]](#cite_note-52) that are members of the Federal Reserve System, [bank holding companies](/wiki/Bank_holding_companies "Bank holding companies") (companies that control banks), the foreign activities of member banks, the U.S. activities of foreign banks, and [Edge Act](/wiki/Edge_Act "Edge Act") and "agreement corporations" (limited-purpose institutions that engage in a foreign banking business). The Board and, under delegated authority, the Federal Reserve Banks, supervise approximately 900 state member banks and 5,000 bank holding companies. Other federal agencies also serve as the primary federal supervisors of commercial banks; the [Office of the Comptroller of the Currency](/wiki/Office_of_the_Comptroller_of_the_Currency "Office of the Comptroller of the Currency") supervises national banks, and the [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation") supervises [state banks](/wiki/State_bank_(United_States) "State bank (United States)") that are not members of the Federal Reserve System.
>
> Some regulations issued by the Board apply to the entire banking industry, whereas others apply only to member banks, that is, [state banks](/wiki/State_bank_(United_States) "State bank (United States)") that have chosen to join the Federal Reserve System and national banks, which by law must be members of the System. The Board also issues regulations to carry out major federal laws governing [consumer credit protection](/wiki/Consumer_protection "Consumer protection"), such as the [Truth in Lending](/wiki/Truth_in_Lending_Act "Truth in Lending Act"), [Equal Credit Opportunity](/wiki/Equal_Credit_Opportunity_Act "Equal Credit Opportunity Act"), and [Home Mortgage Disclosure Acts](/wiki/Home_Mortgage_Disclosure_Act "Home Mortgage Disclosure Act"). Many of these consumer protection regulations apply to various lenders outside the banking industry as well as to banks.
>
> Members of the Board of Governors are in continual contact with other policy makers in government. They frequently testify before [congressional committees](/wiki/Congressional_committee "Congressional committee") on the economy, [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy"), [banking supervision and regulation](/wiki/Bank_regulation "Bank regulation"), [consumer credit protection](/wiki/Consumer_protection "Consumer protection"), [financial markets](/wiki/Financial_market "Financial market"), and other matters.
>
> The Board has regular contact with members of the President's [Council of Economic Advisers](/wiki/Council_of_Economic_Advisers "Council of Economic Advisers") and other key economic officials. The Chair also meets from time to time with the [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and has regular meetings with the [Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/Secretary_of_the_Treasury "Secretary of the Treasury"). The Chair has formal responsibilities in the international arena as well.

The Federal Banking Agency Audit Act, enacted in 1978 as Public Law 95-320 and 31 U.S.C. section 714 establish that the board of governors of the Federal Reserve System and the Federal Reserve banks may be audited by the [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office") (GAO).[[51]](#cite_note-53) The GAO has authority to audit check-processing, currency storage and shipments, and some regulatory and bank examination functions – though there are restrictions to what the GAO may audit.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

##### Regulatory and oversight responsibilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Regulatory and oversight responsibilities")]

The board of directors of each Federal Reserve Bank District also has regulatory and supervisory responsibilities. If the board of directors of a district bank has judged that a member bank is performing or behaving poorly, it will report this to the board of governors. This policy is described in law:

> Each Federal reserve bank shall keep itself informed of the general character and amount of the loans and investments of its member banks with a view to ascertaining whether undue use is being made of bank credit for the speculative carrying of or trading in securities, real estate, or commodities, or for any other purpose inconsistent with the maintenance of sound credit conditions; and, in determining whether to grant or refuse advances, rediscounts, or other credit accommodations, the Federal reserve bank shall give consideration to such information. The chairman of the Federal reserve bank shall report to the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System any such undue use of bank credit by any member bank, together with his recommendation. Whenever, in the judgment of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, any member bank is making such undue use of bank credit, the Board may, in its discretion, after reasonable notice and an opportunity for a hearing, suspend such bank from the use of the credit facilities of the Federal Reserve System and may terminate such suspension or may renew it from time to time.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

### National payments system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: National payments system")]

In the [Depository Institutions Deregulation and Monetary Control Act](/wiki/Depository_Institutions_Deregulation_and_Monetary_Control_Act "Depository Institutions Deregulation and Monetary Control Act") of 1980, Congress reaffirmed that the Federal Reserve should promote an efficient nationwide [payments system](/wiki/Payment_system "Payment system"). The act subjects all depository institutions, not just member commercial banks, to reserve requirements and grants them equal access to Reserve Bank payment services. The twelve Federal Reserve Banks provide banking services to depository institutions and to the federal government. For depository institutions, they maintain accounts and provide various payment services, including collecting checks, electronically transferring funds, and distributing and receiving currency and coin. For the federal government, the Reserve Banks act as fiscal agents, paying Treasury checks; processing electronic payments; and issuing, transferring, and redeeming U.S. government securities.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

The Federal Reserve plays a role in the nation's retail and wholesale payments systems by providing financial services to depository institutions. Retail payments are generally for relatively small-dollar amounts and often involve a depository institution's retail clients. The Reserve Banks' [retail banking services](/wiki/Retail_banking "Retail banking") include distributing currency and coin, collecting checks, electronically transferring funds through [FedACH](/wiki/FedACH "FedACH") (the Federal Reserve's [automated clearing house](/wiki/Automated_clearing_house "Automated clearing house") system), and beginning in 2023, facilitating [instant payments](/wiki/Instant_payment "Instant payment") using the [FedNow](/wiki/FedNow "FedNow") service. The Reserve Banks' [wholesale banking services](/wiki/Wholesale_banking "Wholesale banking") include electronically transferring funds through the [Fedwire Funds Service](/wiki/Fedwire "Fedwire") and transferring securities issued by the U.S. government, its agencies, and certain other entities through the [Fedwire Securities Service](/wiki/Fedwire_Securities_Service "Fedwire Securities Service"). Unlike retail services, wholesale payments are generally for large-dollar amounts and often involve a depository institution's large corporate customers or counterparties.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/FederalReserve_System.png/500px-FederalReserve_System.png)](/wiki/File:FederalReserve_System.png)

Organization of the Federal Reserve System

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Structure")]

Main article: [Structure of the Federal Reserve System](/wiki/Structure_of_the_Federal_Reserve_System "Structure of the Federal Reserve System")

The Federal Reserve System has a "unique structure that is both public and private" and is described as "[independent within the government](/wiki/Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_government "Independent agencies of the United States government")" rather than "[independent of government](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization")".[[11]](#cite_note-auto-12)[[56]](#cite_note-58) The System does not draw upon public funding, and derives its authority and purpose from the [Federal Reserve Act](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act "Federal Reserve Act"), which was passed by Congress in 1913 and is subject to Congressional modification or repeal.[[57]](#cite_note-FRBPhilPubPriv-59) The four main components of the Federal Reserve System are the board of governors, the Federal Open Market Committee, the twelve regional Federal Reserve Banks, and the member banks throughout the country.

### Board of governors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Board of governors")]

Main article: [Federal Reserve Board of Governors](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Board_of_Governors "Federal Reserve Board of Governors")

The seven-member board of governors is a large federal agency that functions in business oversight by examining national banks.[[58]](#cite_note-60)**: 12, 15** It is charged with the overseeing of the 12 District Reserve Banks and setting national monetary policy. It also supervises and regulates the U.S. banking system in general.[[59]](#cite_note-61) Governors are appointed by the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and confirmed by the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") for staggered 14-year terms.[[41]](#cite_note-dfeverydayecon-43)[[60]](#cite_note-62) One term begins every two years, on February 1 of even-numbered years, and members serving a full term cannot be renominated for a second term.[[61]](#cite_note-FRB01-63) "[U]pon the expiration of their terms of office, members of the Board shall continue to serve until their successors are appointed and have qualified." The law provides for the removal of a member of the board by the president of the United States "for cause".[[62]](#cite_note-usc12-242-64) The board is required to make an annual report of operations to the Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives.

The chair and vice chair of the board of governors are appointed by the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") from among the sitting governors. They both serve a four-year term and they can be renominated as many times as the president chooses, until their terms on the board of governors expire.[[63]](#cite_note-usc12-241-65)

### Federal Open Market Committee

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Federal Open Market Committee")]

Main article: [Federal Open Market Committee](/wiki/Federal_Open_Market_Committee "Federal Open Market Committee")

The [Federal Open Market Committee](/wiki/Federal_Open_Market_Committee "Federal Open Market Committee") (FOMC) consists of 12 members, seven from the board of governors and five from the regional Federal Reserve Bank presidents, and must obtain consensus on all decisions. The FOMC oversees and sets policy on [open market operations](/wiki/Open_market_operations "Open market operations"), the principal tool of national monetary policy. The FOMC also directs operations undertaken by the Federal Reserve in foreign exchange markets. All Regional Reserve Bank presidents contribute to the committee's assessment of the economy and of policy options, but only the five presidents who are then members of the FOMC vote on policy decisions. The FOMC determines its own internal organization and, by tradition, elects the chair of the board of governors as its chair and the president of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York as its vice chair. Formal meetings typically are held eight times each year in Washington, D.C.[[66]](#cite_note-70)

There is very strong consensus among economists against politicising the FOMC.[[67]](#cite_note-igmchicago.org-71)

### Federal Advisory Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Federal Advisory Council")]

Main article: [Federal Advisory Council](/wiki/Federal_Advisory_Council "Federal Advisory Council")

The Federal Advisory Council (FAC) is a statutory body established under the [Federal Reserve Act](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act "Federal Reserve Act") of 1913 to provide the [Board of Governors](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Board_of_Governors "Federal Reserve Board of Governors") of the Federal Reserve System with insights and recommendations from the banking industry and regional economic perspectives. Comprising one representative from each of the 12 Federal Reserve Districts, the Council meets at least four times annually in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") to discuss economic and banking issues and offer advisory opinions to the Board. Each [Federal Reserve Bank’s](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank "Federal Reserve Bank") board of directors selects its district’s representative, typically a senior executive from a member bank, ensuring diverse geographic and institutional input.[[6]](#cite_note-initial-6)

### Federal Reserve Banks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Federal Reserve Banks")]

Main article: [Federal Reserve Bank](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank "Federal Reserve Bank")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Federal_Reserve_Districts_Map_-_Banks_%26_Branches.png/500px-Federal_Reserve_Districts_Map_-_Banks_%26_Branches.png)](/wiki/File:Federal_Reserve_Districts_Map_-_Banks_%26_Branches.png)

Map of the 12 Federal Reserve Districts, with the 12 Federal Reserve Banks marked as black squares, and all Branches within each district (24 total) marked as red circles. The Washington, DC, headquarters is marked with a star. (Also, a 25th branch in Buffalo, NY, was closed in 2008.)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/US_Federal_Reserve_Banks_collage_1936.jpg/500px-US_Federal_Reserve_Banks_collage_1936.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Federal_Reserve_Banks_collage_1936.jpg)

The 12 Reserve Banks buildings in 1936

There are 12 [Federal Reserve Banks](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank "Federal Reserve Bank"), each of which is responsible for member banks located in its district. They are located in [Boston](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Boston "Federal Reserve Bank of Boston"), [New York](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_New_York "Federal Reserve Bank of New York"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Philadelphia "Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Cleveland "Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland"), [Richmond](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Richmond "Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond"), [Atlanta](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Atlanta "Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta"), [Chicago](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Chicago "Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago"), [St. Louis](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_St._Louis "Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis"), [Minneapolis](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Minneapolis "Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis"), [Kansas City](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Kansas_City "Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City"), [Dallas](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Dallas "Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas"), and [San Francisco](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_San_Francisco "Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco"). The size of each district was set based upon the population distribution of the United States when the Federal Reserve Act was passed. The charter and organization of each bank is established by law and cannot be altered by the member banks.[[41]](#cite_note-dfeverydayecon-43)[[68]](#cite_note-R000107-72) Each regional bank has a president, who is the chief executive officer of their bank. Each president is nominated by their bank's board of directors, but the nomination is contingent upon approval by the board of governors. Presidents serve five-year terms and may be reappointed.[[69]](#cite_note-FRB_pres-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Member_Federal_Reserve_System_plaque.png/250px-Member_Federal_Reserve_System_plaque.png)](/wiki/File:Member_Federal_Reserve_System_plaque.png)

Plaque marking a bank as a member

### Member banks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Member banks")]

A member bank is a private institution and owns stock in its regional Federal Reserve Bank. All nationally chartered banks hold stock in one of the Federal Reserve Banks. State chartered banks may choose to be members (and hold stock in their regional Federal Reserve bank) upon meeting certain standards. The amount of stock a member bank must own is equal to 3% of its combined capital and surplus.[[70]](#cite_note-74) About 38% of U.S. banks are members of their regional Federal Reserve Bank.[[11]](#cite_note-auto-12)[[71]](#cite_note-75)

Holding stock in a Federal Reserve bank is not like owning stock in a publicly traded company. These stocks cannot be sold or traded, and member banks do not control the Federal Reserve Bank as a result of owning this stock. From their Regional Bank, member banks with $10 billion or less in assets receive a dividend of 6%, while member banks with more than $10 billion in assets receive the lesser of 6% or the current 10-year Treasury auction rate.[[72]](#cite_note-76) The remainder of the regional Federal Reserve Banks' profits is given over to the [United States Treasury Department](/wiki/United_States_Treasury_Department "United States Treasury Department"). In 2015, the Federal Reserve Banks made a profit of $100.2 billion and distributed $2.5 billion in dividends to member banks as well as returning $97.7 billion to the U.S. Treasury.[[24]](#cite_note-frs_bog_net_income_press_lease-26)

### Accountability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Accountability")]

An external auditor selected by the audit committee of the Federal Reserve System regularly audits the Board of Governors and the Federal Reserve Banks. The GAO will audit some activities of the Board of Governors. These audits do not cover "most of the Fed's monetary policy actions or decisions, including discount window lending, open-market operations and any other transactions made under the direction of the Federal Open Market Committee" ...[nor may the GAO audit] "dealings with foreign governments and other central banks."[[73]](#cite_note-77)

The annual and quarterly financial statements prepared by the Federal Reserve System conform to a basis of accounting that is set by the Federal Reserve Board and does not conform to [Generally Accepted Accounting Principles](/wiki/Generally_Accepted_Accounting_Principles_(United_States) "Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (United States)") (GAAP) or government [Cost Accounting Standards](/wiki/Cost_Accounting_Standards "Cost Accounting Standards") (CAS). The financial reporting standards are defined in the Financial Accounting Manual for the Federal Reserve Banks.[[74]](#cite_note-AccountingManual-78) The cost accounting standards are defined in the Planning and Control System Manual.[[74]](#cite_note-AccountingManual-78) As of 27 August 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit), the Federal Reserve Board has been publishing unaudited financial reports for the Federal Reserve banks every quarter.[[75]](#cite_note-79)

On November 7, 2008, [Bloomberg L.P.](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P.") brought [a lawsuit](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P._v._Board_of_Governors_of_the_Federal_Reserve_System "Bloomberg L.P. v. Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System") against the board of governors of the Federal Reserve System to force the board to reveal the identities of firms for which it provided guarantees during the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[[76]](#cite_note-80) Bloomberg, L.P. won at the trial court[[77]](#cite_note-81) and the Fed's appeals were rejected at both the [United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit") and the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court"). The data was released on March 31, 2011.[[78]](#cite_note-82)

## Monetary policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Monetary policy")]

Further information: [Monetary policy of the United States](/wiki/Monetary_policy_of_the_United_States "Monetary policy of the United States") and [Fractional-reserve banking](/wiki/Fractional-reserve_banking "Fractional-reserve banking")

The term "[monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy")" refers to the actions undertaken by a central bank, such as the Federal Reserve, to influence economic activity (the [overall demand](/wiki/Aggregate_demand "Aggregate demand") for goods and services) to help promote national economic goals. The [Federal Reserve Act of 1913](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act_of_1913 "Federal Reserve Act of 1913") gave the Federal Reserve authority to set monetary policy in the United States. The Fed's mandate for monetary policy is commonly known as the dual mandate of promoting maximum employment and stable prices, the latter being interpreted as a stable inflation rate of 2 percent per year on average. The Fed's monetary policy influences economic activity by influencing the general level of [interest rates](/wiki/Interest_rate "Interest rate") in the economy, which again via the [monetary transmission mechanism](/wiki/Monetary_transmission_mechanism "Monetary transmission mechanism") affects households' and firms' demand for goods and services and in turn employment and inflation.[[36]](#cite_note-goals_of_MP-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Unemployment_vs_Inflation_vs_Inverted_yield_curve.webp/500px-Unemployment_vs_Inflation_vs_Inverted_yield_curve.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Unemployment_vs_Inflation_vs_Inverted_yield_curve.webp)

[Unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment_in_the_United_States "Unemployment in the United States") vs [Inflation](/wiki/United_States_Consumer_Price_Index "United States Consumer Price Index") vs [Inverted yield curve](/wiki/Inverted_yield_curve "Inverted yield curve")

Unemployment rate

Inflation [CPI](/wiki/United_States_Consumer_Price_Index "United States Consumer Price Index")

10 year [bond](/wiki/United_States_Treasury_security "United States Treasury security") minus 2 year bond [inverted yield curve](/wiki/Inverted_yield_curve "Inverted yield curve")

### Interbank lending

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Interbank lending")]

The Federal Reserve sets monetary policy by influencing the [federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate"), which is the rate of interbank lending of [reserve balances](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Deposits "Federal Reserve Deposits"). The rate that banks charge each other for these loans is determined in the [interbank market](/wiki/Interbank_lending_market "Interbank lending market"), and the Federal Reserve influences this rate through the "tools" of monetary policy described in the [*Tools* section](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_System#Monetary_policy "Federal Reserve System") below. The federal funds rate is a short-term interest rate that the FOMC focuses on, which affects the longer-term interest rates throughout the economy. The Federal Reserve explained the implementation of its monetary policy in 2021:

> The FOMC has the ability to influence the federal funds rate–and thus the cost of short-term interbank credit–by changing the rate of interest the Fed pays on reserve balances that banks hold at the Fed. A bank is unlikely to lend to another bank (or to any of its customers) at an interest rate lower than the rate that the bank can earn on reserve balances held at the Fed. And because overall reserve balances are currently abundant, if a bank wants to borrow reserve balances, it likely will be able to do so without having to pay a rate much above the rate of interest paid by the Fed.[[36]](#cite_note-goals_of_MP-38)

Changes in the target for the federal funds rate affect overall financial conditions through various channels, including subsequent changes in the market interest rates that commercial banks and other lenders charge on short-term and longer-term loans, and changes in [asset prices](/wiki/Asset_price "Asset price") and in currency [exchange rates](/wiki/Exchange_rate "Exchange rate"), which again affects [private consumption](/wiki/Private_consumption "Private consumption"), [investment](/wiki/Investment "Investment") and [net export](/wiki/Net_export "Net export"). By easening or tightening the stance of monetary policy, i.e. lowering or raising its target for the federal funds rate, the Fed can either spur or restrain growth in the overall US demand for goods and services.[[36]](#cite_note-goals_of_MP-38)

### Tools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Tools")]

There are four main tools of monetary policy that the Federal Reserve uses to implement its monetary policy:[[79]](#cite_note-Fed-83)[[80]](#cite_note-84)

* Interest on reserve balances (IORB)
  + Interest paid on funds that banks hold in their reserve balance accounts at their Federal Reserve Bank.[[81]](#cite_note-85) IORB is the primary tool for moving the federal funds rate within the target range.[[79]](#cite_note-Fed-83)
* Overnight reverse repurchase agreement (ON RRP) facility
  + The Fed's standing offer to many large nonbank financial institutions to deposit funds at the Fed and earn interest. Acts as a supplementary tool for moving the FFR within the target range.[[79]](#cite_note-Fed-83)
* [Open market operations](/wiki/Open_market_operations "Open market operations")
  + Purchases and sales of U.S. Treasury and federal agency securities. Used to maintain an ample supply of reserves.[[79]](#cite_note-Fed-83)
* [Discount window](/wiki/Discount_window "Discount window")
  + The Fed's lending to banks at the discount rate.[[82]](#cite_note-86) Helps put a ceiling on the FFR.[[79]](#cite_note-Fed-83)

#### Federal funds rate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Federal funds rate")]

Further information: [Federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Federal_funds_rate_history_and_recessions.png/960px-Federal_funds_rate_history_and_recessions.png)](/wiki/File:Federal_funds_rate_history_and_recessions.png)

The Federal Reserve System implements [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") largely by targeting the [federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate"). This is the [interest rate](/wiki/Interest_rate "Interest rate") that banks charge each other for overnight loans of [federal funds](/wiki/Federal_funds "Federal funds"), which are the reserves held by banks at the Fed. This rate is actually determined by the market and is not explicitly mandated by the Fed. The Fed therefore tries to align the effective federal funds rate with the targeted rate, mainly by adjusting its IORB rate.[[83]](#cite_note-RIP_MM-87) The Federal Reserve System usually adjusts the federal funds rate target by 0.25% or 0.50% at a time.

#### Interest on reserve balances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Interest on reserve balances")]

The interest on reserve balances (IORB) is the interest that the Fed pays on funds held by commercial banks in their reserve balance accounts at the individual Federal Reserve System banks. It is an administrated interest rate (i.e. set directly by the Fed as opposed to a [market interest rate](/wiki/Market_interest_rate "Market interest rate") which is determined by the forces of supply and demand).[[83]](#cite_note-RIP_MM-87) As banks are unlikely to lend their reserves in the FFR market for less than they get paid by the Fed, the IORB guides the effective FFR and is used as the primary tool of the Fed's monetary policy.[[84]](#cite_note-new_tools-88)[[83]](#cite_note-RIP_MM-87)

#### Open market operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Open market operations")]

Further information: [Open market operations](/wiki/Open_market_operations "Open market operations")

Open market operations are done through the sale and purchase of [United States Treasury securities](/wiki/United_States_Treasury_securities "United States Treasury securities"), or "Treasurys". The Federal Reserve buys Treasurys both directly and via [primary dealers](/wiki/Primary_dealers "Primary dealers"), which have accounts at depository institutions.[[85]](#cite_note-FRB_of_NY-89)

The Federal Reserve's objective for open market operations has varied over the years. During the 1980s, the focus gradually shifted toward attaining a specified level of the [federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate") (the rate that banks charge each other for overnight loans of federal funds, which are the reserves held by banks at the Fed), a process that was largely complete by the end of the decade.[[86]](#cite_note-90)

Until the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), the Fed used open market operations as its primary tool to adjust the supply of reserve balances in order to keep the federal funds rate around the Fed's target.[[87]](#cite_note-91) This regime is also known as a limited reserves regime.[[84]](#cite_note-new_tools-88) After the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), the Federal Reserve has adopted a so-called ample reserves regime where open market operations leading to modest changes in the supply of reserves are no longer effective in influencing the FFR. Instead the Fed uses its administered rates, in particular the IORB rate, to influence the FFR.[[84]](#cite_note-new_tools-88)[[83]](#cite_note-RIP_MM-87) However, open market operations are still an important maintenance tool in the overall framework of the conduct of monetary policy as they are used for ensuring that reserves remain ample.[[84]](#cite_note-new_tools-88)

##### Repurchase agreements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Repurchase agreements")]

Further information: [Repurchase agreement](/wiki/Repurchase_agreement "Repurchase agreement")

To smooth temporary or cyclical changes in the money supply, the desk engages in [repurchase agreements](/wiki/Repurchase_agreement "Repurchase agreement") (repos) with its primary dealers. Repos are essentially secured, short-term lending by the Fed. On the day of the transaction, the Fed deposits money in a primary dealer's reserve account, and receives the promised securities as [collateral](/wiki/Collateral_(finance) "Collateral (finance)"). When the transaction matures, the process unwinds: the Fed returns the collateral and charges the primary dealer's reserve account for the principal and accrued interest. The term of the repo (the time between settlement and maturity) can vary from 1 day (called an overnight repo) to 65 days.[[88]](#cite_note-92)

#### Discount window and discount rate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Discount window and discount rate")]

Further information: [Discount window](/wiki/Discount_window "Discount window")

The Federal Reserve System also directly sets the **discount rate**, which is the interest rate for "discount window lending", overnight loans that member banks borrow directly from the Fed. This rate is generally set at a rate close to 100 [basis points](/wiki/Basis_point "Basis point") above the target federal funds rate. The idea is to encourage banks to seek alternative funding before using the "discount rate" option.[[89]](#cite_note-93) The equivalent operation by the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") is referred to as the "[marginal lending facility](/wiki/Marginal_lending_facility "Marginal lending facility")".[[90]](#cite_note-94)

Both the discount rate and the federal funds rate influence the [prime rate](/wiki/Prime_rate "Prime rate"), which is usually about 3 percentage points higher than the federal funds rate.

#### Term Deposit facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Term Deposit facility")]

The Term Deposit facility is a program through which the Federal Reserve Banks offer interest-bearing [term deposits](/wiki/Term_deposits "Term deposits") to eligible institutions.[[91]](#cite_note-95) It is intended to facilitate the implementation of monetary policy by providing a tool by which the Federal Reserve can manage the aggregate quantity of reserve balances held by depository institutions. Funds placed in term deposits are removed from the accounts of participating institutions for the life of the term deposit and thus drain reserve balances from the banking system. The program was announced December 9, 2009, and approved April 30, 2010, with an effective date of June 4, 2010.[[92]](#cite_note-96) Fed Chair Ben S. Bernanke, testifying before the House Committee on Financial Services, stated that the Term Deposit Facility would be used to reverse the expansion of credit during the Great Recession, by drawing funds out of the money markets into the Federal Reserve Banks.[[93]](#cite_note-97) It would therefore result in increased market interest rates, acting as a brake on economic activity and inflation.[[94]](#cite_note-98) The Federal Reserve authorized up to five "small-value offerings" in 2010 as a pilot program.[[95]](#cite_note-99) After three of the offering auctions were successfully completed, it was announced that small-value auctions would continue on an ongoing basis.[[96]](#cite_note-100)

#### Quantitative easing (QE) policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Quantitative easing (QE) policy")]

A little-used tool of the Federal Reserve is the quantitative easing policy.[[97]](#cite_note-101) Under that policy, the Federal Reserve buys back corporate bonds and mortgage backed securities held by banks or other financial institutions. This in effect puts money back into the financial institutions and allows them to make loans and conduct normal business.
The bursting of the [United States housing bubble](/wiki/United_States_housing_bubble "United States housing bubble") prompted the Fed to buy mortgage-backed securities for the first time in November 2008. Over six weeks, a total of $1.25 trillion were purchased in order to stabilize the housing market, about one-fifth of all U.S. government-backed mortgages.[[98]](#cite_note-102)

### Expired policy tools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Expired policy tools")]

#### Reserve requirements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Reserve requirements")]

An instrument of monetary policy adjustment historically employed by the Federal Reserve System was the fractional [reserve requirement](/wiki/Reserve_requirement "Reserve requirement"), also known as the required reserve ratio.[[99]](#cite_note-103) The required reserve ratio set the balance that the Federal Reserve System required a depository institution to hold in the Federal Reserve Banks.[[100]](#cite_note-BoG_2005_pp=27-104) The required reserve ratio was set by the board of governors of the Federal Reserve System.[[101]](#cite_note-105) The reserve requirements have changed over time and some history of these changes is published by the Federal Reserve.[[102]](#cite_note-hist_resreq-106)

As a response to the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), the Federal Reserve started making interest payments on depository institutions' required and excess reserve balances. The payment of interest on excess reserves gave the central bank greater opportunity to address credit market conditions while maintaining the federal funds rate close to the target rate set by the FOMC.[[103]](#cite_note-107) The reserve requirement did not play a significant role in the post-2008 interest-on-excess-reserves regime,[[104]](#cite_note-fed-zero-rr-pr-108) and in March 2020, the reserve ratio was set to zero for all banks, which meant that no bank was required to hold any reserves, and hence the reserve requirement effectively ceased to exist, though the legal framework exists for it to be reinstated at any time.[[1]](#cite_note-fed-zero-rr-1)[[105]](#cite_note-109)

#### Temporary policy tools during the 2008 financial crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Temporary policy tools during the 2008 financial crisis")]

In order to address problems related to the [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis") and [United States housing bubble](/wiki/United_States_housing_bubble "United States housing bubble"), several new tools were created. The first new tool, called the [Term auction facility](/wiki/Term_auction_facility "Term auction facility"), was added on December 12, 2007. It was announced as a temporary tool,[[106]](#cite_note-taffaq-110) but remained in place for a prolonged period of time.[[107]](#cite_note-111) Creation of the second new tool, called the [Term Securities Lending Facility](/wiki/Term_Securities_Lending_Facility "Term Securities Lending Facility"), was announced on March 11, 2008.[[108]](#cite_note-tslfannounce-112) The main difference between these two facilities was that the Term auction Facility was used to inject cash into the banking system whereas the Term securities Lending Facility was used to inject [treasury securities](/wiki/Treasury_securities "Treasury securities") into the banking system.[[109]](#cite_note-113) Creation of the third tool, called the [Primary Dealer Credit Facility](/wiki/Primary_Dealer_Credit_Facility "Primary Dealer Credit Facility") (PDCF), was announced on March 16, 2008.[[110]](#cite_note-114) The PDCF was a fundamental change in Federal Reserve policy because it enabled the Fed to lend directly to [primary dealers](/wiki/Primary_dealer "Primary dealer"), which was previously against Fed policy.[[111]](#cite_note-115) The differences between these three facilities was described by the Federal Reserve:[[112]](#cite_note-pdcffaq-116)

> The Term auction Facility program offers term funding to depository institutions via a bi-weekly auction, for fixed amounts of credit. The Term securities Lending Facility will be an auction for a fixed amount of lending of Treasury general collateral in exchange for OMO-eligible and AAA/Aaa rated private-label residential mortgage-backed securities. The Primary Dealer Credit Facility now allows eligible primary dealers to borrow at the existing Discount Rate for up to 120 days.

Some measures taken by the Federal Reserve to address the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") had not been used since the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[113]](#cite_note-117)

#### Term auction facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Term auction facility")]

Further information: [Term auction facility](/wiki/Term_auction_facility "Term auction facility")

The Term auction Facility was a program in which the Federal Reserve auctioned term funds to depository institutions.[[106]](#cite_note-taffaq-110) The creation of this facility was announced by the Federal Reserve on December 12, 2007, and was done in conjunction with the [Bank of Canada](/wiki/Bank_of_Canada "Bank of Canada"), the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank"), and the [Swiss National Bank](/wiki/Swiss_National_Bank "Swiss National Bank") to address elevated pressures in short-term funding markets.[[114]](#cite_note-taf-118) The reason it was created was that banks were not lending funds to one another and banks in need of funds were refusing to go to the discount window. Banks were not lending money to each other because there was a fear that the loans would not be paid back. Banks refused to go to the discount window because it was usually associated with the stigma of bank failure.[[115]](#cite_note-119)[[116]](#cite_note-120)[[117]](#cite_note-121)[[118]](#cite_note-122) Under the Term auction Facility, the identity of the banks in need of funds was protected in order to avoid the stigma of bank failure.[[119]](#cite_note-mwtaf-123) [Foreign exchange swap lines](/wiki/Central_bank_liquidity_swap "Central bank liquidity swap") with the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") and [Swiss National Bank](/wiki/Swiss_National_Bank "Swiss National Bank") were opened so the banks in Europe could have access to [U.S. dollars](/wiki/U.S._dollar "U.S. dollar").[[119]](#cite_note-mwtaf-123) The final Term Auction Facility auction was carried out on March 8, 2010.[[120]](#cite_note-124)

#### Term securities lending facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Term securities lending facility")]

The Term securities Lending Facility was a 28-day facility that offered Treasury general collateral to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York's primary dealers in exchange for other program-eligible collateral. It was intended to promote liquidity in the financing markets for Treasury and other collateral and thus to foster the functioning of financial markets more generally.[[121]](#cite_note-tslffaq-125) Like the Term auction Facility, the TSLF was done in conjunction with the [Bank of Canada](/wiki/Bank_of_Canada "Bank of Canada"), the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank"), and the [Swiss National Bank](/wiki/Swiss_National_Bank "Swiss National Bank"). The resource allowed dealers to switch debt that was less liquid for U.S. government securities that were easily tradable. The currency swap lines with the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") and [Swiss National Bank](/wiki/Swiss_National_Bank "Swiss National Bank") were increased. The TSLF was closed on February 1, 2010.[[122]](#cite_note-126)

#### Primary dealer credit facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Primary dealer credit facility")]

The Primary Dealer Credit Facility (PDCF) was an overnight loan facility that provided funding to primary dealers in exchange for a specified range of eligible collateral and was intended to foster the functioning of financial markets more generally.[[112]](#cite_note-pdcffaq-116) The PDCF established in 2008 was closed on February 1, 2010, alongside other crisis-era facilities.[[123]](#cite_note-127) A new PDCF was introduced in March 2020 in response to COVID-19-related market disruptions, and that facility ceased extending credit in 2021.[[124]](#cite_note-128)

#### Asset Backed Commercial Paper Money Market Mutual Fund Liquidity Facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Asset Backed Commercial Paper Money Market Mutual Fund Liquidity Facility")]

The Asset Backed Commercial Paper Money Market Mutual Fund Liquidity Facility (ABCPMMMFLF) was also called the AMLF. The Facility began operations on September 22, 2008, and was closed on February 1, 2010.[[125]](#cite_note-129) All U.S. depository institutions, bank holding companies (parent companies or U.S. broker-dealer affiliates), or U.S. branches and agencies of foreign banks were eligible to borrow under this facility pursuant to the discretion of the FRBB.

Collateral eligible for pledge under the Facility was required to meet the following criteria:

* was purchased by Borrower on or after September 19, 2008, from a registered investment company that held itself out as a money market mutual fund;
* was purchased by Borrower at the Fund's acquisition cost as adjusted for amortization of premium or accretion of discount on the ABCP through the date of its purchase by Borrower;
* was rated at the time pledged to FRBB, not lower than A1, F1, or P1 by at least two major rating agencies or, if rated by only one major rating agency, the ABCP must have been rated within the top rating category by that agency;
* was issued by an entity organized under the laws of the United States or a political subdivision thereof under a program that was in existence on September 18, 2008; and
* had stated maturity that did not exceed 120 days if the Borrower was a bank or 270 days for non-bank Borrowers.

#### Commercial Paper Funding Facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Commercial Paper Funding Facility")]

On October 7, 2008, the Federal Reserve further expanded the collateral it would loan against to include commercial paper using the [Commercial Paper Funding Facility](/wiki/Commercial_Paper_Funding_Facility "Commercial Paper Funding Facility") (CPFF). The action made the Fed a crucial source of credit for non-financial businesses in addition to commercial banks and investment firms. Fed officials said they would buy as much of the debt as necessary to get the market functioning again. They refused to say how much that might be, but they noted that around $1.3 trillion worth of commercial paper would qualify. There was $1.61 trillion in outstanding commercial paper, seasonally adjusted, on the market as of 1 October 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit), according to the most recent data from the Fed. That was down from $1.70 trillion in the previous week. Since the summer of 2007, the market had shrunk from more than $2.2 trillion.[[126]](#cite_note-130)[[127]](#cite_note-131) This program lent out a total $738 billion before it was closed. Forty-five out of 81 of the companies participating in this program were foreign firms. Research shows that [Troubled Asset Relief Program](/wiki/Troubled_Asset_Relief_Program "Troubled Asset Relief Program") (TARP) recipients were twice as likely to participate in the program than other commercial paper issuers who did not take advantage of the TARP bailout. The Fed incurred no losses from the CPFF.[[128]](#cite_note-132)

In response to the economic disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, the Federal Reserve reintroduced the Commercial Paper Funding Facility (CPFF) on March 17, 2020, to support the flow of credit to households and businesses by purchasing eligible commercial paper.[[129]](#cite_note-133) The CPFF was modeled after the 2008 crisis-era facility and aimed to stabilize the commercial paper market.[[130]](#cite_note-Commercial_Paper_Funding_Facility-134) The facility ceased operations on March 31, 2021, and is not in place as of April 2025.[[130]](#cite_note-Commercial_Paper_Funding_Facility-134)

#### Term Asset-Backed Security Loan Facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Term Asset-Backed Security Loan Facility")]

The [Term Asset-Backed Securities Loan Facility](/wiki/Term_Asset-Backed_Securities_Loan_Facility "Term Asset-Backed Securities Loan Facility") (TALF) was a temporary program announced on November 25, 2008, and launched in March 2009 by the Federal Reserve in collaboration with the [U.S. Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") to stimulate consumer and business lending. It provided non-recourse loans to investors to purchase [asset-backed securities](/wiki/Asset-backed_security "Asset-backed security") (ABS), such as [auto loans](/wiki/Car_finance "Car finance"), [student loans](/wiki/Student_loan "Student loan"), and credit card receivables, aiming to enhance liquidity in these markets.[[131]](#cite_note-135) The facility ceased issuing new loans in June 2010 and was fully wound down by 2015. As a significant tool during the 2008 financial crisis, TALF focused on ABS markets rather than direct banking system liquidity, distinguishing it from other crisis-era facilities.[[132]](#cite_note-136)
