### Central banking in the United States, 1791–1913

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Central banking in the United States, 1791–1913")]

Main article: [History of central banking in the United States](/wiki/History_of_central_banking_in_the_United_States "History of central banking in the United States")

The first attempt at a national currency was during the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). In 1775, the Continental Congress, as well as the states, began issuing paper currency, calling the bills "[Continentals](/wiki/Early_American_currency "Early American currency")".[[134]](#cite_note-138) The Continentals were backed only by future tax revenue, and were used to help finance the Revolutionary War. Overprinting, as well as British counterfeiting, caused the value of the Continental to diminish quickly. This experience with paper money led the United States to strip the power to issue Bills of Credit (paper money) from a draft of the new Constitution on August 16, 1787,[[135]](#cite_note-139) as well as banning such issuance by the various states, and limiting the states' ability to make anything but gold or silver coin legal tender on August 28.[[136]](#cite_note-140)

In 1791, the government granted the [First Bank of the United States](/wiki/First_Bank_of_the_United_States "First Bank of the United States") a charter to operate as the U.S. central bank until 1811.[[137]](#cite_note-A_Brief_History_of_Our_Nation's_Paper_Money-141) The First Bank of the United States came to an end under [President Madison](/wiki/President_Madison "President Madison") when Congress refused to renew its charter. The [Second Bank of the United States](/wiki/Second_Bank_of_the_United_States "Second Bank of the United States") was established in 1816, and lost its authority to be the central bank of the U.S. twenty years later under [President Jackson](/wiki/President_Jackson "President Jackson") when its charter expired. Both banks were based upon the Bank of England.[[138]](#cite_note-142) Ultimately, a third national bank, known as the Federal Reserve, was established in 1913 and still exists to this day.

#### First Central Bank, 1791 and Second Central Bank, 1816

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: First Central Bank, 1791 and Second Central Bank, 1816")]

The first U.S. institution with central banking responsibilities was the [First Bank of the United States](/wiki/First_Bank_of_the_United_States "First Bank of the United States"), chartered by Congress and signed into law by President [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") on February 25, 1791, at the urging of [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"). This was done despite strong opposition from [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") and [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"), among numerous others. The charter was for twenty years and expired in 1811 under President Madison, when Congress refused to renew it.[[139]](#cite_note-boshistory-143)

In 1816, however, Madison revived it in the form of the [Second Bank of the United States](/wiki/Second_Bank_of_the_United_States "Second Bank of the United States"). Years later, early renewal of the bank's charter became the primary issue in the reelection of President [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson"). After Jackson, who was opposed to the central bank, was reelected, he pulled the government's funds out of the bank. Jackson was the only President to completely pay off the national debt[[140]](#cite_note-144) but his efforts to close the bank contributed to the [Panic of 1837](/wiki/Panic_of_1837 "Panic of 1837"). The bank's charter was not renewed in 1836, and it would fully dissolve after several years as a private corporation.

From 1837 to 1862, in the [Free Banking Era](/wiki/Free_Banking_Era "Free Banking Era") there was no formal central bank. From 1846 to 1921, an [Independent Treasury System](/wiki/Independent_Treasury_System "Independent Treasury System") ruled.
From 1863 to 1913, a system of national banks was instituted by the 1863 [National Banking Act](/wiki/National_Banking_Act "National Banking Act") during which a series of bank panics, in [1873](/wiki/Panic_of_1873 "Panic of 1873"), [1893](/wiki/Panic_of_1893 "Panic of 1893"), and [1907](/wiki/Panic_of_1907 "Panic of 1907") occurred.[[8]](#cite_note-FDS-H-04-8)[[9]](#cite_note-FDS-H-05-9)[[10]](#cite_note-FDS-H-06-10)

#### Creation of Third Central Bank, 1907–1913

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Creation of Third Central Bank, 1907–1913")]

Main article: [History of the Federal Reserve System](/wiki/History_of_the_Federal_Reserve_System "History of the Federal Reserve System")

The main motivation for the third central banking system came from the [Panic of 1907](/wiki/Panic_of_1907 "Panic of 1907"), which caused a renewed desire among legislators, economists, and bankers for an overhaul of the monetary system.[[8]](#cite_note-FDS-H-04-8)[[9]](#cite_note-FDS-H-05-9)[[10]](#cite_note-FDS-H-06-10)[[141]](#cite_note-herrickpanic-145) During the last quarter of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th century, the United States economy went through a series of [financial panics](/wiki/Financial_panics "Financial panics").[[142]](#cite_note-EFlaherty-146) According to many economists, the previous national banking system had two main weaknesses: an [inelastic](/wiki/Inelastic "Inelastic") currency and a lack of liquidity.[[142]](#cite_note-EFlaherty-146) In 1908, Congress enacted the [Aldrich–Vreeland Act](/wiki/Aldrich%E2%80%93Vreeland_Act "Aldrich–Vreeland Act"), which provided for an emergency currency and established the [National Monetary Commission](/wiki/National_Monetary_Commission "National Monetary Commission") to study banking and currency reform.[[143]](#cite_note-mnwarburg-147) The National Monetary Commission returned with recommendations which were repeatedly rejected by Congress. A revision crafted during a secret meeting on [Jekyll Island](/wiki/Jekyll_Island "Jekyll Island") by Senator Aldrich and representatives of the nation's top finance and industrial groups later became the basis of the [Federal Reserve Act](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act "Federal Reserve Act").[[144]](#cite_note-148)

##### Federal Reserve Act, 1913

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Federal Reserve Act, 1913")]

Main article: [Federal Reserve Act](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act "Federal Reserve Act")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Fed_Reserve.JPG/500px-Fed_Reserve.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fed_Reserve.JPG)

Newspaper clipping, December 24, 1913

The head of the bipartisan National Monetary Commission was financial expert and Senate [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") leader [Nelson Aldrich](/wiki/Nelson_Aldrich "Nelson Aldrich"). Aldrich set up two commissions – one to study the American monetary system in depth and the other, headed by Aldrich himself, to study the European central banking systems and report on them.[[143]](#cite_note-mnwarburg-147)

In early November 1910, Aldrich met with five well known members of the New York banking community to devise a central banking bill. [Paul Warburg](/wiki/Paul_Warburg "Paul Warburg"), an attendee of the meeting and longtime advocate of central banking in the U.S., later wrote that Aldrich was "bewildered at all that he had absorbed abroad and he was faced with the difficult task of writing a highly technical bill while being harassed by the daily grind of his parliamentary duties".[[145]](#cite_note-Warburg_document-149) After ten days of deliberation, the bill, which would later be referred to as the "Aldrich Plan", was agreed upon. It had several key components, including a central bank with a Washington-based headquarters and fifteen branches located throughout the U.S. in geographically strategic locations, and a uniform elastic currency based on gold and commercial paper. Aldrich believed a central banking system with no political involvement was best, but was convinced by Warburg that a plan with no public control was not politically feasible.[[145]](#cite_note-Warburg_document-149) The compromise involved representation of the public sector on the board of directors.[[146]](#cite_note-ecresearch-150)

Aldrich's bill met much opposition from politicians. Critics charged Aldrich of being biased due to his close ties to wealthy bankers such as [J. P. Morgan](/wiki/J._P._Morgan "J. P. Morgan") and [John D. Rockefeller Jr.](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller_Jr. "John D. Rockefeller Jr."), Aldrich's son-in-law. Most Republicans favored the Aldrich Plan,[[146]](#cite_note-ecresearch-150) but it lacked enough support in Congress to pass because rural and western states viewed it as favoring the "eastern establishment".[[5]](#cite_note-mnglass-5)[[147]](#cite_note-151) In contrast, progressive Democrats favored a reserve system owned and operated by the government; they believed that public ownership of the central bank would end Wall Street's control of the American currency supply.[[146]](#cite_note-ecresearch-150) Conservative Democrats fought for a privately owned, yet decentralized, reserve system, which would still be free of Wall Street's control.[[146]](#cite_note-ecresearch-150)

The original Aldrich Plan was dealt a fatal blow in 1912, when Democrats won the White House and Congress.[[145]](#cite_note-Warburg_document-149) Nonetheless, President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") believed that the Aldrich plan would suffice with a few modifications. The plan became the basis for the Federal Reserve Act, which was proposed by Senator [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_L._Owen "Robert L. Owen") in May 1913. The primary difference between the two bills was the transfer of control of the board of directors (called the Federal Open Market Committee in the Federal Reserve Act) to the government.[[5]](#cite_note-mnglass-5)[[139]](#cite_note-boshistory-143) The bill passed Congress on December 23, 1913,[[148]](#cite_note-152) on a mostly partisan basis, with most Democrats voting "yea" and most Republicans voting "nay".[[139]](#cite_note-boshistory-143)

The House voted on December 22, 1913, with 298 voting yes to 60 voting no. The Senate voted 43–25 on December 23, 1913.[[149]](#cite_note-153) President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") signed the bill later that day.[[150]](#cite_note-154)

### Federal Reserve era, 1913–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Federal Reserve era, 1913–present")]

Main article: [History of the Federal Reserve](/wiki/History_of_the_Federal_Reserve "History of the Federal Reserve")

Key laws affecting the Federal Reserve have been:[[151]](#cite_note-155)

* [Federal Reserve Act](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act "Federal Reserve Act"), 1913
* [Glass–Steagall Act](/wiki/Glass%E2%80%93Steagall_Act "Glass–Steagall Act"), 1933
* [Banking Act of 1935](/wiki/Banking_Act_of_1935 "Banking Act of 1935")
* [Employment Act of 1946](/wiki/Employment_Act_of_1946 "Employment Act of 1946")
* [Federal Reserve-Treasury Department Accord of 1951](/wiki/1951_Accord "1951 Accord")
* [Bank Holding Company Act of 1956](/wiki/Bank_Holding_Company_Act_of_1956 "Bank Holding Company Act of 1956") and the amendments of 1970
* [Federal Reserve Reform Act of 1977](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Reform_Act_of_1977 "Federal Reserve Reform Act of 1977")
* [International Banking Act of 1978](/wiki/International_Banking_Act_of_1978 "International Banking Act of 1978")
* [Full Employment and Balanced Growth Act](/wiki/Full_Employment_and_Balanced_Growth_Act "Full Employment and Balanced Growth Act") (1978)
* [Depository Institutions Deregulation and Monetary Control Act](/wiki/Depository_Institutions_Deregulation_and_Monetary_Control_Act "Depository Institutions Deregulation and Monetary Control Act") (1980)
* [Financial Institutions Reform, Recovery and Enforcement Act of 1989](/wiki/Financial_Institutions_Reform,_Recovery_and_Enforcement_Act_of_1989 "Financial Institutions Reform, Recovery and Enforcement Act of 1989")
* [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation Improvement Act of 1991](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation_Improvement_Act_of_1991 "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation Improvement Act of 1991")
* [Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act](/wiki/Gramm%E2%80%93Leach%E2%80%93Bliley_Act "Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act") (1999)
* [Financial Services Regulatory Relief Act](/w/index.php?title=Financial_Services_Regulatory_Relief_Act&action=edit&redlink=1 "Financial Services Regulatory Relief Act (page does not exist)") (2006)
* [Emergency Economic Stabilization Act](/wiki/Emergency_Economic_Stabilization_Act "Emergency Economic Stabilization Act") (2008)
* [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act") (2010)

The Banking Act of 1935 created the modern structure of the Federal Reserve and placed monetary decisions beyond presidential control, thus enshrining the [independence](/wiki/Central_bank_independence "Central bank independence") of the Federal Reserve.[[152]](#cite_note-156)

## Economic data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Economic data")]

The Federal Reserve records and publishes large amounts of data, including the Board of Governors' Economic Data and Research page,[[153]](#cite_note-157) Statistical Releases and Historical Data Page,[[154]](#cite_note-158) and the St. Louis Fed's FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data) page.[[155]](#cite_note-159) The [Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC)](/wiki/Federal_Open_Market_Committee "Federal Open Market Committee") examines many economic indicators prior to determining monetary policy.[[156]](#cite_note-eco_ind-160) Some economists have criticised the economic data compiled by the Fed. The Fed sponsors much of the monetary economics research in the U.S., and [Lawrence H. White](/wiki/Lawrence_H._White "Lawrence H. White") objects that this makes it less likely for researchers to publish findings challenging the status quo.[[157]](#cite_note-161)

### Net worth of households and nonprofit organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Net worth of households and nonprofit organizations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Total_Net_Worth_-_Balance_Sheet_of_Households_and_Nonprofit_Organizations_1949-2012.jpg/960px-Total_Net_Worth_-_Balance_Sheet_of_Households_and_Nonprofit_Organizations_1949-2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Total_Net_Worth_-_Balance_Sheet_of_Households_and_Nonprofit_Organizations_1949-2012.jpg)

Total Net Worth‍—‌Balance Sheet of Households and Nonprofit Organizations 1949–2012

The net worth of households and nonprofit organizations in the United States is published by the Federal Reserve in a report titled *Flow of Funds*.[[158]](#cite_note-162) At the end of the third quarter of fiscal year 2012, this value was $64.8 trillion. At the end of the first quarter of fiscal year 2014, this value was $95.5 trillion.[[159]](#cite_note-163) As of the fourth quarter of 2024, the net worth of households and nonprofit organizations reached $172.7 trillion, driven primarily by gains in corporate equity and real estate values.[[160]](#cite_note-164)

### Money supply

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Money supply")]

Further information: [Money supply](/wiki/Money_supply "Money supply")

The most common measures are named M0 (narrowest), M1, M2, and M3. In the United States they are defined by the Federal Reserve as follows:

| Measure | Definition |
| --- | --- |
| M0 | The total of all physical [currency](/wiki/Currency "Currency"), plus accounts at the central bank that can be exchanged for physical currency. |
| M1 | M0 + those portions of M0 held as reserves or vault cash + the amount in [demand accounts](/wiki/Demand_account "Demand account") ("checking" or "current" accounts). |
| M2 | M1 + most [savings accounts](/wiki/Savings_account "Savings account"), [money market accounts](/wiki/Money_market_account "Money market account"), and small denomination time deposits ([certificates of deposit](/wiki/Certificates_of_deposit "Certificates of deposit") of under $100,000). |
| M3 | M2 + all other CDs, deposits of [eurodollars](/wiki/Eurodollars "Eurodollars") and [repurchase agreements](/wiki/Repurchase_agreement "Repurchase agreement"). |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Components_of_the_United_States_money_supply2.svg/500px-Components_of_the_United_States_money_supply2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Components_of_the_United_States_money_supply2.svg)

The Federal Reserve stopped publishing M3 statistics in March 2006, saying that the data cost a lot to collect but did not provide significantly useful information.[[161]](#cite_note-prxliv-165) The other three money supply measures continue to be provided in detail.

### Personal consumption expenditures price index

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Personal consumption expenditures price index")]

Further information: [Personal consumption expenditures price index](/wiki/Personal_consumption_expenditures_price_index "Personal consumption expenditures price index")

The [personal consumption expenditures price index](/wiki/Personal_consumption_expenditures_price_index "Personal consumption expenditures price index"), also referred to as simply the PCE price index, is used as one measure of the value of money. It is a United States-wide indicator of the average increase in prices for all domestic personal consumption. Using a variety of data including [United States Consumer Price Index](/wiki/United_States_Consumer_Price_Index "United States Consumer Price Index") and [U.S. Producer Price Index](/wiki/U.S._Producer_Price_Index "U.S. Producer Price Index") prices, it is derived from the largest component of the [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") in the BEA's [National Income and Product Accounts](/wiki/National_Income_and_Product_Accounts "National Income and Product Accounts"), personal consumption expenditures.

One of the Fed's main roles is to maintain price stability, which means that the Fed's ability to keep a low inflation rate is a long-term measure of their success.[[162]](#cite_note-166) Although the Fed is not required to maintain inflation within a specific range, their long run target for the growth of the PCE price index is between 1.5 and 2 percent.[[163]](#cite_note-167) There has been debate among policy makers as to whether the Federal Reserve should have a specific [inflation targeting](/wiki/Inflation_targeting "Inflation targeting") policy.[[164]](#cite_note-168)

#### Inflation and the economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Inflation and the economy")]

Most [mainstream economists](/wiki/Mainstream_economists "Mainstream economists") favor a low, steady rate of inflation.[[165]](#cite_note-econjournalwatch.org-169) [Chief economist](/wiki/Chief_economist "Chief economist"), and advisor to the Federal Reserve, the [Congressional Budget Office](/wiki/Congressional_Budget_Office "Congressional Budget Office") and the [Council of Economic Advisers](/wiki/Council_of_Economic_Advisers "Council of Economic Advisers"),[[166]](#cite_note-170)[[167]](#cite_note-171) [Diane C. Swonk](/wiki/Diane_C._Swonk "Diane C. Swonk") observed, in 2022, that "From the Fed's perspective, you have to remember inflation is kind of like cancer. If you don't deal with it now with something that may be painful, you could have something that metastasized and becomes much more chronic later on."[[168]](#cite_note-172)

Low (as opposed to zero or [negative](/wiki/Deflation "Deflation")) inflation may reduce the severity of economic [recessions](/wiki/Recession "Recession") by enabling the labor market to adjust more quickly in a downturn, and reduce the risk that a [liquidity trap](/wiki/Liquidity_trap "Liquidity trap") prevents [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") from stabilizing the economy.[[169]](#cite_note-173) The task of keeping the rate of inflation low and stable is usually given to [monetary authorities](/wiki/Monetary_authority "Monetary authority").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/US_Seasonal_Unemployment.svg/500px-US_Seasonal_Unemployment.svg.png)](/wiki/File:US_Seasonal_Unemployment.svg)

United States unemployment rates 1975–2010 showing variance between the fifty states

## Budget

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Budget")]

The Federal Reserve is self-funded. Over 90 % of Fed revenues come from open market operations, specifically the interest on the portfolio of Treasury securities as well as "capital gains/losses" that may arise from the buying/selling of the securities and their derivatives as part of Open Market Operations. The balance of revenues come from sales of financial services (check and electronic payment processing) and discount window loans.[[170]](#cite_note-174)

The Federal Reserve Board creates a budget report once per year for Congress. There are two reports with budget information. The one that lists the complete balance statements with income and expenses, as well as the net profit or loss, is the large report simply titled, "Annual Report". It also includes data about employment throughout the system. The other report, which explains in more detail the expenses of the different aspects of the whole system, is called "Annual Report: Budget Review".[[171]](#cite_note-budget-175)

### Remittance payments to the Treasury

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Remittance payments to the Treasury")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Remittances.webp/960px-Remittances.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Remittances.webp)

Federal Reserve remittances to the [U.S. Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Treasury "United States Treasury") (annually)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Federal_Reserve_Remittences_to_the_Treasury.webp/960px-Federal_Reserve_Remittences_to_the_Treasury.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Federal_Reserve_Remittences_to_the_Treasury.webp)

Federal Reserve Remittances to the [Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Treasury "United States Treasury") (weekly)

The Federal Reserve has been [remitting](/wiki/Remitting "Remitting") interest that it has been receiving back to the [United States Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Treasury "United States Treasury"). Most of the assets the Fed holds are [U.S. Treasury bonds](/wiki/United_States_Treasury_security "United States Treasury security") and [mortgage-backed securities](/wiki/Mortgage-backed_securities "Mortgage-backed securities") that it has been purchasing as part of [quantitative easing](/wiki/Quantitative_easing "Quantitative easing") since the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"). In 2022, the Fed started [quantitative tightening](/wiki/Quantitative_tightening "Quantitative tightening") (QT) and selling these assets and taking a loss on them in the [secondary](/wiki/Secondary_market "Secondary market") [bond market](/wiki/Bond_market "Bond market"). As a result, the nearly $100 billion that it was remitting annually to the Treasury, is expected to be discontinued during QT.[[172]](#cite_note-176)[[173]](#cite_note-177)

In 2023, the Federal Reserve reported a net negative income of $114.3 billion.[[174]](#cite_note-178) This triggered the creation of a deferred asset liability on the Federal Reserve balance sheet booked as "Interest on Federal Reserve notes due to U.S. Treasury" totaling $133.3 billion.[[175]](#cite_note-179) The deferred asset is the amount of net excess revenues the Federal Reserve must realize before remittances can continue. It does not have any impact on the ability of the Federal Reserve to conduct monetary policy or meet its obligations.[[176]](#cite_note-180) The Federal Reserve has estimated the deferred asset will last until mid-2027.[[177]](#cite_note-181)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a0/Consolidated_Statement_of_Condition_of_All_Federal_Reserve_Banks-LIABILITIES.png)](/wiki/File:Consolidated_Statement_of_Condition_of_All_Federal_Reserve_Banks-LIABILITIES.png)

Total combined liabilities for all 12 Federal Reserve Banks, 2007–2009

One of the keys to understanding the Federal Reserve is the Federal Reserve balance sheet (or [balance statement](/wiki/Balance_sheet "Balance sheet")). In accordance with Section 11 of the [Federal Reserve Act](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Act "Federal Reserve Act"), the [board of governors](/wiki/Board_of_governors "Board of governors") of the Federal Reserve System publishes once each week the "Consolidated Statement of Condition of All Federal Reserve Banks" showing the condition of each Federal Reserve bank and a consolidated statement for all Federal Reserve banks. The board of governors requires that excess earnings of the Reserve Banks be transferred to the Treasury as interest on Federal Reserve notes.[[178]](#cite_note-182)

The Federal Reserve releases its balance sheet every Thursday.[[179]](#cite_note-183) Below is the balance sheet as of 8 April 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit) (in billions of dollars):

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Reserve&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Main articles: [Criticism of the Federal Reserve](/wiki/Criticism_of_the_Federal_Reserve "Criticism of the Federal Reserve") and [Causes of the Great Depression](/wiki/Causes_of_the_Great_Depression "Causes of the Great Depression")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/FFR_treasuries.webp/960px-FFR_treasuries.webp.png)](/wiki/File:FFR_treasuries.webp)

[30 year mortgage average](/wiki/Mortgage_loan "Mortgage loan")

[30 Year Treasury Bond](/wiki/Treasury_bond "Treasury bond")

10 Year Treasury Bond

2 Year Treasury Bond

3 month Treasury Bond

Effective Federal Funds Rate

[CPI inflation](/wiki/United_States_Consumer_Price_Index "United States Consumer Price Index") year/year

  [Recessions](/wiki/List_of_recessions_in_the_United_States "List of recessions in the United States")

The Federal Reserve System has faced various criticisms since its inception in 1913. Some of the most common critiques focus on its monetary policy, lack of transparency, and its potential role in exacerbating financial instability.[[181]](#cite_note-bi-ronpaul-185) Critics argue that the Fed’s expansionary policies—such as lowering interest rates and increasing the money supply—can lead to inflation, asset bubbles, and economic distortions. Prominent economists like [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman") have criticized the Fed for contributing to economic downturns, including its role in the Great Depression.[[29]](#cite_note-:0-31) [Libertarian](/wiki/Libertarianism_in_the_United_States "Libertarianism in the United States") figures such as [Ron Paul](/wiki/Ron_Paul "Ron Paul") have been especially vocal in calling for greater accountability and transparency within the Fed, advocating for measures such as auditing the Federal Reserve to ensure it serves the public interest rather than benefiting large financial institutions. Additionally, some critics, including [Rand Paul](/wiki/Rand_Paul "Rand Paul"), argue that the Fed disproportionately serves the interests of the banking elite, given the backgrounds of many of its officials in finance and banking, leading to potential conflicts of interest and policies that favor [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") over the general economy.

Another area of criticism is the Federal Reserve’s departure from the [gold standard](/wiki/Gold_standard "Gold standard") in 1971, which many argue has contributed to long-term inflationary pressures and a devaluation of the U.S. dollar. Ron Paul believes the Fed should be [abolished](/wiki/End_the_Fed "End the Fed") and replaced with a return to the [gold standard](/wiki/Gold_standard "Gold standard").[[32]](#cite_note-:1-34) Advocates of [Austrian economics](/wiki/Austrian_school_of_economics "Austrian school of economics"), such as [Ludwig von Mises](/wiki/Ludwig_von_Mises "Ludwig von Mises") and [Murray Rothbard](/wiki/Murray_Rothbard "Murray Rothbard"), believe that the move to fiat currency destabilized the monetary system and undermined financial stability.[[182]](#cite_note-186) The Federal Reserve’s handling of the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") has also been a focal point of criticism, with some arguing that the Fed’s response—[bailing out large banks](/wiki/Emergency_Economic_Stabilization_Act_of_2008 "Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008") and financial institutions—created [moral hazard](/wiki/Moral_hazard "Moral hazard") and worsened the economic collapse.[[183]](#cite_note-187)

During [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States"), the Federal Reserve's policies such as increasing its [bank reserves](/wiki/Bank_reserves "Bank reserves"), [quantitative easing (QE)](/wiki/Quantitative_easing "Quantitative easing"), and keeping interest rates near zero until March 2022 had been criticised by economists, especially [monetarists](/wiki/Monetarism "Monetarism"), as greatly contributing to the inflation spike which peaked at a record high in half a century.[[184]](#cite_note-188)[[185]](#cite_note-189)[[186]](#cite_note-190)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Ron_Paul%2C_official_Congressional_photo_portrait%2C_2007.jpg/250px-Ron_Paul%2C_official_Congressional_photo_portrait%2C_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ron_Paul,_official_Congressional_photo_portrait,_2007.jpg)

[Ron Paul](/wiki/Ron_Paul "Ron Paul"), a longtime critic of the Federal Reserve, argues in *[End the Fed](/wiki/End_the_Fed "End the Fed")* that the central bank undermines economic stability, enables inflation, and should be abolished in favor of a [free-market monetary system](/wiki/Hard_currency "Hard currency").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Money_supply_during_the_great_depression_era.png/960px-Money_supply_during_the_great_depression_era.png)](/wiki/File:Money_supply_during_the_great_depression_era.png)

Money supply decreased significantly between [Black Tuesday](/wiki/Black_Tuesday "Black Tuesday") and the [Bank Holiday in March 1933](/wiki/Emergency_Banking_Act "Emergency Banking Act") when there were massive [bank runs](/wiki/Bank_runs "Bank runs") across the United States.

117. **[^](#cite_ref-121)** ["A dirty job, but someone has to do it"](http://www.economist.com/displaystory.cfm?story_id=10286586). *economist.com*. December 13, 2007. Retrieved August 29, 2011. "The Fed's discount window, for instance, through which it lends direct to banks, has barely been approached, despite the soaring spreads in the interbank market. The quarter-point cuts in its federal funds rate and discount rate on December 11 were followed by a steep sell-off in the stockmarket...The hope is that by extending the maturity of central-bank money, broadening the range of collateral against which banks can borrow and shifting from direct lending to an auction, the central bankers will bring down spreads in the one- and three-month [money markets](/wiki/Money_market "Money market"). There will be no net addition of liquidity. What the central bankers add at longer-term maturities, they will take out in the overnight market. But there are risks. The first is that, for all the fanfare, the central banks' plan will make little difference. After all, it does nothing to remove the fundamental reason why investors are worried about lending to banks. This is the uncertainty about potential losses from subprime mortgages and the products based on them, and – given that uncertainty – the banks' own desire to hoard capital against the chance that they will have to strengthen their balance sheets."

* Hafer, R. W. *The Federal Reserve System: An Encyclopedia*. Greenwood Press, 2005. 451 pp, 280 entries; [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-313-32839-0](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-313-32839-0 "Special:BookSources/0-313-32839-0").

* [Meyer, Laurence H.](/wiki/Laurence_Meyer "Laurence Meyer") (2004). *A Term at the Fed: An Insider's View*. [HarperBusiness](/wiki/HarperBusiness "HarperBusiness"). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-06-054270-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-06-054270-5 "Special:BookSources/0-06-054270-5"); focuses on the period from 1996 to 2002, emphasizing [Alan Greenspan](/wiki/Alan_Greenspan "Alan Greenspan")'s chairmanship during the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"), the [stock market boom](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble") and the [financial aftermath](/wiki/September_11,_2001_attacks#Economic_aftermath "September 11, 2001 attacks") of the September 11, 2001, attacks.
* Woodward, Bob. *Maestro: Greenspan's Fed and the American Boom* (2000) study of Greenspan in the 1990s.
